title: What's the Difference Between a College and a University?
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You say 'I'm going to college.' Your friend says 'I'm going to university.' You're both talking about higher education. But the words are not interchangeable. The difference affects class sizes, research opportunities, graduate programs, and campus culture. Here is what you need to know before you choose.

The Simple Definition

A college typically offers undergraduate degrees only. A university offers both undergraduate and graduate degrees (master's and doctoral). That's the technical difference.

But there's more to it. Many schools called 'colleges' are actually universities (Boston College, Dartmouth College). Many schools called 'universities' started as colleges. The name doesn't always tell the full story.

Liberal arts colleges are a specific category. They focus on undergraduate teaching. Class sizes are small. Professors teach, not graduate students. Research is secondary to teaching. Examples: Williams, Amherst, Swarthmore.

Universities have graduate schools. They conduct research. Professors may spend as much time on research as teaching. Undergraduate classes can be large, especially introductory courses. Examples: Harvard, Stanford, University of Michigan.

Class Sizes

At a liberal arts college, your largest class might have 40 students. Your smallest might have 8. You'll know your professors. They'll know your name.

At a large university, introductory classes can have 300 students. You'll sit in a lecture hall with a teaching assistant running discussion sections. Your professor may not know who you are until you visit office hours.

Upper-level courses at universities are smaller. By junior year, classes sizes may resemble those at a liberal arts college. But the first two years are different.

Teaching vs. Research

At liberal arts colleges, professors are hired to teach. Their research is expected but secondary. They will spend time on your paper, meet with you outside class, and write detailed recommendations.

At research universities, professors are hired to research. They bring in grants. They publish papers. They may teach one or two courses per semester. Some are excellent teachers. Some are not. Teaching assistants (graduate students) often lead discussion sections and grade papers.

If you want to be taught by leading researchers, go to a university. If you want to be taught by dedicated teachers, go to a liberal arts college. Both have excellent teachers. The emphasis differs.

Graduate Programs and Research Opportunities

Universities have graduate students. You can work alongside PhD candidates. You can get research experience that prepares you for graduate school.

Liberal arts colleges have few or no graduate students. Undergraduates get research opportunities directly with professors. You won't compete with graduate students for attention.

If you plan to go to graduate school, both paths work. Liberal arts colleges send many students to top PhD programs. Universities have more research infrastructure. Both can prepare you well.

Campus Culture

Liberal arts colleges are often residential. Students live on campus all four years. The community is tight-knit. Everyone knows everyone (for better and worse).

Universities are often commuter-friendly. Students may live off-campus after freshman year. The community is larger and more diverse. You can find your niche, but you can also get lost.

Which One Is Right for You?

Choose a liberal arts college if:
• You want small classes and personal attention
• You're not sure what you want to study (liberal arts are designed for exploration)
• You want to be taught by professors, not teaching assistants
• You prefer a close-knit community

Choose a university if:
• You want a wider range of majors and courses
• You're interested in research (especially in STEM)
• You want the energy of a large, diverse campus
• You're already focused on a specific major (like engineering or business)

The best fit depends on you. Visit both types. Sit in on classes. Talk to students. The right environment is the one where you'll learn best.
